data.title: The existing dilemmas of prediction markets
data.description:
Author: Spencer Farrar Source: X, @SpencerFarrar Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

During the holidays, a surfing accident left me with a head injury, confining me to the sofa all day. Never one for resting, this sedentary lifestyle was unbearable. Naturally, my brother and I became avid participants in the prediction market. Now, news and sports events are my primary sources of information, and the prediction market's order book has become our arena.

We trade everything: from tail-end geopolitical risks like the probability of a US invasion of Venezuela (I opened a short position and barely made a profit at the end of the year), to NBA game results, nothing is overlooked.

Ultimately, we devised a strategy that multiplied our account balance 12-fold in 10 days—the core of which was identifying undervalued assets with pricing discrepancies, where the order book's trading direction was excessively biased towards the more popular side. The price difference between Las Vegas betting odds and prediction market order books is actually quite easy to calculate. For example, we bet nearly 10:1 on the Minnesota Timberwolves to defeat the Oklahoma City Thunder. If you've watched Anthony Edwards' recent games, you know his performance has been virtually unstoppable; this asset clearly suffered from a fundamental pricing error. Furthermore, we studied arbitrage opportunities across platforms; excluding settlement risk, some opportunities could yield nearly 10% "risk-free" returns. However, in the process of observing real-time changes in liquidity and risk adjustments, the various vulnerabilities of the current prediction market infrastructure were also exposed. While "collective intelligence" is a poetic concept, the current development of prediction markets is facing a structural ceiling. The Current State of the Market: The primary obstacle facing prediction markets today is the lack of deep liquidity. Liquidity is the bridge connecting theoretical prices and tradable prices; it ensures that a high-confidence trade delivers new information to the market, rather than simply disrupting the order book. If a prediction market has little liquidity, it is merely a place for a few to speculate and gamble. To become a true financial instrument, a prediction market must have deep liquidity, allowing large investors to move millions of dollars in trades without unexpectedly causing large price fluctuations. Liquidity and the reliability of trade execution are the core of trading infrastructure. Market makers who cannot quickly adjust bid-ask spreads in a deep order book are easily targeted by arbitrageurs; and when market makers cannot hedge or price accurately, market liquidity disappears instantly. Currently, the liquidity of prediction markets is mostly concentrated in the sports event sector. This is because designated contract market licenses (only 20 issued nationwide) allow licensees to conduct sports betting prediction transactions outside of states where sports betting is legal. To transcend the scope of sports betting, prediction markets must address the problem of harmful trading flows. The very nature of prediction markets incentivizes participants with insider information to engage in asymmetric betting. In traditional stock markets, insider trading is illegal; however, in prediction markets, it is a price discovery mechanism. This creates a hostile trading environment for market makers: if market makers know they are consistently outmaneuvered by insider traders, they will either widen their bid-ask spreads to reduce risk or exit the market altogether. This is what the industry calls the adverse selection problem. Furthermore, a major obstacle to the professionalization of prediction markets is capital inefficiency. Currently, most prediction markets use a full-collateralization model (1:1 margin ratio): to bet $100, you must deposit $100 in margin. This model is an anomaly in the professional trading field. In traditional derivatives markets such as perpetual swaps and futures, traders can use leverage to trade without tying up all their capital. Without leverage, the potential return on equity in prediction markets is limited, making it difficult to attract priority investment. Moreover, the lack of a margin system makes closing positions more difficult. In a full-collateralization market, investors cannot close losing positions through net settlement unless they find a buyer willing to pay the full face value; otherwise, they are forced to hold the high-cost binary bet contract until expiration. This capital-locked model is completely impractical for high-frequency market makers who need to circulate funds every millisecond. Where does the future of prediction markets lie? To become a multi-trillion dollar asset class, prediction markets must evolve towards the Lloyd's of London model, becoming a trading market where professional institutions compete to underwrite unique, high-risk assets. The value of prediction markets lies not only in predicting events like election results, but also in their ability to visualize and trade specific risks related to a entity's financial situation that were previously unquantifiable. Imagine this scenario: Apple can specifically hedge against supply chain risks caused by fluctuations in TSMC's production capacity; the price discovery process for these niche risks is open to the public, allowing the market to more accurately price the stability of business operations. Perhaps the greatest value of prediction markets lies in transmitting price signals to the global market. When prediction markets enable businesses to hedge specific survival risks, they transform from tools for retail speculation into a core component of financial infrastructure. The Paradox of Counterparty Risk However, this evolution of prediction markets faces a final hurdle: counterparty risk. A former colleague who once built a derivatives trading department at a large bank reminded me that in high-risk financial transactions, traders sometimes prefer to face explicit counterparty risk—knowing who to sue in case of default. Transactions with regulated banks with a history of government bailouts (i.e., the implicit guarantee of "too big to fail") provide both psychological and legal security. This raises a core question for the new generation of traders and corporate hedgers: would you rather face the settlement risk of decentralized protocols like Uma and Polymarket, or would you prefer to transact with a primary bank? While many believe that code is law, institutional markets still prefer clearly defined responsible parties. For prediction markets to reach the scale of the global derivatives market, they must bridge the gap between the trustless efficiency of blockchain and the legal recourse of traditional finance. Standardized Trading Rules To address this issue, prediction markets need to emulate the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA). The ISD's Master Agreement is considered the "holy grail" of finance. This standardized contract clearly stipulates how to handle various contingencies such as default, bankruptcy, and settlement disputes. Global market participants pre-accept these rules, eliminating the need for intermediaries to determine transaction outcomes. Currently, prediction markets are beginning to break away from two traditional models: one is the centralized processing model, where platforms like Kalshi or a single committee determine transaction outcomes; the other is the social oracle model, where decentralized protocols like UMA determine the truth of events through voting. The future of prediction markets lies in standardized event contracts. In these contracts, settlement criteria (such as specific data from the UK Office for Labour Statistics or filings with the Federal Court) are hard-coded into the contract structure. By abandoning centralized processing institutions and human oracles, prediction market contracts will possess the same reliability as yen swap contracts in terms of law and finance. Some may object, arguing that event contracts are highly susceptible to defined edge cases and therefore inherently difficult to compare with the standardized protocols of the International Swaps and Derivatives Association. However, standardization allows various events with clear outcomes—elections, referendums, regulatory decisions—to become tradable financial instruments, driving the institutionalization of prediction markets. Events like Brexit and the 2016 US presidential election have triggered enormous economic impacts, but institutional investors lacked direct means to express their views on these events, hedge related risks, or transfer risk. Standardized event contracts transform discrete event outcomes into components of investment portfolios, allowing investors to precisely manage risk exposure, hedge subsequent impacts, and construct event-driven trading strategies using rigorous methods of studying interest rates, foreign exchange, and credit products. This capability will attract new trading demands from asset management institutions, corporations, and risk trading departments. These entities have always had their own judgments on various major events but have struggled with the lack of compliant and convenient trading channels. Only in this way can prediction markets grow into a trillion-dollar asset class. The potential of prediction markets has long existed. The formidable beast now looming ahead is not just Anthony Edwards on the basketball court, but also the massive untapped risk liquidity in the corporate sector, awaiting a sufficiently robust trading venue to take root.

data.title: Understanding Physical AI: Crypto Opportunities Also Hide in Hidden Corners?
data.description:
Author: Haotian; Source: X, @tmel0211

What exactly did Jensen Huang say at the Davos Forum?

On the surface, he was promoting robots, but in reality, he was carrying out a bold "self-revolution." He ended the old era of "stacking graphics cards" with a single statement, but unexpectedly provided a once-in-a-lifetime entry ticket for the Crypto sector?

Yesterday, at the Davos Forum, Huang pointed out that the AI ​​application layer is experiencing explosive growth, and the demand for computing power will shift entirely from the "training side" to the "inference side" and the "Physical AI side." This is quite interesting. Nvidia, as the biggest winner of the "computing power arms race" in the AI ​​1.0 era, is now proactively announcing this shift towards the "inference side" and "Physical AI," which sends a very clear signal: The era of "miracles through brute force"—training large models with massive amounts of GPUs—is over. Future AI competition will revolve around "application is king," focusing on the practical application of AI. In other words, Physical AI is the second half of Generative AI. Because LLM has read all the data that humans have accumulated on the internet over decades, it still doesn't know how to open a bottle cap like a human. Physical AI aims to solve the problem of "unity of knowledge and action" beyond AI intelligence. This is because physical AI cannot rely on the "long reaction time" of remote cloud servers. The logic is simple: if ChatGPT is a second slower in generating text, you'll only feel a lag, but if a bipedal robot is a second slower due to network latency, it might fall down the stairs. However, while Physical AI seems to be a continuation of generative AI, it actually faces three completely different new challenges: 1) Spatial intelligence: Enabling AI to understand the three-dimensional world. Professor Li Feifei once proposed that spatial intelligence is the next North Star in the evolution of AI. For a robot to move, it must first "understand" its environment. This is not merely recognizing "this is a chair," but understanding "the chair's position and structure in three-dimensional space, and how much force I need to use to move it." This requires massive amounts of real-time 3D environmental data covering every corner of the indoor and outdoor environment; 2) Virtual training ground: allowing AI to learn through trial and error in a simulated world. The Omniverse mentioned by Jensen Huang is actually a kind of "virtual training ground." Before entering the real physical world, robots need to be trained in a virtual environment to "fall down 10,000 times" to learn to walk. This process is called Sim-to-Real, that is, from simulation to reality. If robots were to directly experiment and fail in reality, the hardware costs would be astronomical. This process places exponential demands on the throughput of the physics engine's simulation and rendering capabilities. 3) Electronic Skin: "Tactile Data"—A Gold Mine to be Tapped. For Physical AI to possess "tactile feedback," it needs electronic skin to sense temperature, pressure, and texture. This "tactile data" is a completely new asset that has never been collected on a large scale before. This likely requires large-scale sensor data collection. At CES, Ensuring showcased a "mass-produced skin" with 1,956 sensors integrated into a single, densely packed hand, enabling the robot to peel eggs with incredible ease. This "tactile data" is a completely new asset that has never been collected on a large scale before. After seeing this, you'll definitely feel that the emergence of the Physical AI discourse has given many wearable devices and humanoid robots a significant opportunity to shine. Keep in mind that just a few years ago, these were largely criticized as "oversized toys." Actually, what I want to say is that within the new landscape of Physical AI, the Crypto sector also presents an excellent opportunity to fill ecological gaps. Here are a few examples: 1. AI giants can send street view cars to scan every main street in the world, but they can't collect data from the nooks and crannies of streets, neighborhoods, and basements. However, by using token incentives provided by DePIN network devices, and encouraging global users to fill these gaps with their personal devices, it's possible to fill this gap. 2. As mentioned earlier, robots cannot rely on cloud computing power, but they need to utilize edge computing and distributed rendering capabilities on a large scale in the short term, especially for simulating real-world data. Using distributed computing networks to aggregate idle consumer hardware, distributing and scheduling it, can be very useful. 3. "Tactile data," besides large-scale sensor applications, is inherently extremely private. How can we get the public to share this privacy-sensitive data with AI giants? A feasible path is to ensure that those who contribute data receive data ownership and dividends. In summary: Physical AI represents the second half of the web2 AI landscape, as Huang (Huang Jianlin) has proclaimed. And isn't the same true for web3 AI + Crypto sectors like DePIN, DeAI, and DeData? What do you think?

data.title: Vietnam’s Crypto Licensing Shakeup Could Push Amateur Exchanges Out as Banks Rush In
data.description:
Vietnam Introduces New Crypto Regulation, Effectively Squeezing Out Smaller Exchanges?

Vietnam has opened up itself to accepting cryptocurrency after years-long regulatory ambiguity by introducing a licensing pilot program, bringing digital asset trading platforms under official oversight.

Under Decision No. 96/QD-BTC issued by the Ministry of Finance, authorities established procedures for issuing, adjusting, and revoking licenses, placing exchanges under the supervision of the State Securities Commission.

Vietnam kicks off a 5-year pilot for licensing crypto exchanges — high bar with $380M+ capital requirement, institutional dominance, and strict staffing rules. Moving crypto out of gray area into regulated framework, banks gearing up. Southeast Asia adoption heating up pic.twitter.com/0F7NQAW7oz

— Taurus - Bitcoin Bull (@Taurus4BTC) January 22, 2026

The initiative ends a period where crypto in the country wasn't given any clear legal recognition, signaling a significant shift toward institutionalized, transparent operations.

Major financial institutions are moving fast to capitalize on the new regime. Around 10 securities firms and banks, including SSI Securities, VIX Securities, MBBank, Techcombank, and VPBank, have already announced plans to launch regulated exchanges pending approval. Partnerships with tech firms such as FPT Corp. and international blockchain players like Tether and AWS further indicate institutional commitment to compliant, scalable platforms.

However, the pilot program participants put applicants under a fairly strict requirement under Government Resolution No. 05/2025/NQ-CP. For example, applicants must be Vietnamese enterprises holding a minimum fund of VND10 trillion paid-in capital, largely from institutional investors, and meet rigorous standards for infrastructure, governance, cybersecurity, and licensed staffing.

JUST IN: 🇻🇳 Vietnam launches national blockchain NDAChain to power digital IDs, smart contracts & government records with hybrid decentralization. pic.twitter.com/FiTZpkluqE

— Whale Insider (@WhaleInsider) July 25, 2025

These requirements effectively raise the bar for exchange operators, making it increasingly difficult for small or amateur exchanges to survive. Analysts suggest that many of the previously informal or “mom-and-pop” platforms will either consolidate, partner with regulated entities, or exit the market altogether.

Vietnam’s Market Boom and Global Crypto Interest

The timing of the pilot program coincides with Vietnam’s rapidly expanding crypto market. According to Chainalysis, transaction volumes reached $220–230 billion between July 2024 and June 2025, ranking the country among the top three Asia-Pacific crypto hubs.

JUST IN: Charles Hoskinson says "look at Nigeria, 23% of the population holds crypto and uses crypto. Vietnam, it's massive, it's like 40M people out of 120M, about one out of every three people is doing something with crypto." $ADA$NIGHTpic.twitter.com/u7KrhrjKdt

— Angry Crypto Show (@angrycryptoshow) January 15, 2026

The Law on Digital Technology Industry, effective January 1, 2026, formally brought digital assets under regulatory oversight, establishing a legal foundation for licensed operations.

Global stablecoin issuer Tether has identified Vietnam as a strategic market due to its youthful population, rapid economic growth, and high remittance flows.

South East Asia is leaning hard into RWA.

Vietnam is working directly with @Tether_to 🇻🇳

Malaysia's central bank released its 3-year tokenization framework 🇲🇾

Indonesia just launched it's CBDC 🇮🇩

Powerhouse of a region pic.twitter.com/bxc49nhMkw

— Ray Buckton (@RayBuckton) November 15, 2025

Tether’s Vice President, Marco Dal Lago, offered expertise in building compliant frameworks during meetings with Deputy Prime Minister Ho Duc Phoc, emphasizing sustainable crypto development aligned with Vietnamese regulation.

What This Means for the Future

Vietnam’s licensing pilot signals a new era for crypto in the country: institutional players are set to dominate, while smaller, less compliant exchanges may be forced out, effectively cleaning up the market.

By formalizing rules and requiring significant capital and operational standards, the government aims to attract international investment while maintaining financial stability and protecting users from unregulated platforms.

As Vietnam balances innovation with oversight, the licensing pilot may serve as a model for other emerging markets seeking to integrate crypto responsibly, transforming a chaotic gray-zone market into a professional, competitive, and compliant ecosystem.

data.title: CoinFound Annual Report: TradFi x Crypto 2026 Outlook
data.description:
Takeaway

• 2025 was a year in which traditional finance and Crypto moved from "parallel experiments" to "deep entanglement."

• In 2026, the following macroeconomic trends will impact the TradeFi x Crypto space:

  1. The crisis of trust in the fiat currency system and the return of hard assets: Faced with the global debt spiral and the risk of "fiscal dominance," institutions are accelerating the allocation of "hard assets" such as gold, Bitcoin, and commodities to hedge against the erosion of fiat currency credibility.

  2. Geopolitical drive for the implementation of parallel clearing systems: The need to "deweaponize" financial infrastructure is driving blockchain to become an alternative settlement solution independent of SWIFT, and atomic settlement mechanisms effectively reduce trust and counterparty risks in cross-border transactions.

  3. AI Productivity Monetization and the Rise of Machine Payments: AI investment is shifting its focus from computing hardware to economic productivity generation, creating a rigid demand for compliant stablecoins and on-chain automatic settlement by AI Agents to achieve value confirmation between machines. Energy Becomes a Core Asset and Mining Companies Become Infrastructure-Driven: Power shortages are prompting mining companies to transform into "hybrid computing centers." Their scarce power access rights (Time-to-Power) are triggering mergers and acquisitions by tech giants, driving mining company valuations towards data center infrastructure. On-Chain Assets (RWA) Moving from Issuance to Utility: Asset tokenization is entering the "programmable finance" stage. RWA is no longer just a digital certificate, but serves as a 24/7 core collateral, significantly improving the capital efficiency of the repurchase market and overall liquidity. Private lending faces stress tests and a transformation towards transparency: A wave of debt maturing in 2026 may trigger default risks, forcing the industry to shift from a "black box" approach to real-time on-chain transparent auditing based on zero-knowledge proofs (ZK) to avoid a chain of liquidations in DeFi. Infrastructure shifts from fragmented competition to consolidation by giants: The market is entering a consolidation period similar to the telecommunications industry, with payment and financial giants acquiring stablecoin middleware and custodians to secure their positions, eliminate redundancy, and build compliance barriers. Emerging markets shift from speculation to structural dependence: Crypto assets have become a fundamental tool for payments and remittances in emerging markets, and the vast real-world user scenarios make them a core hub connecting TradFi assets and global retail liquidity. • The following are the trends expected in 2026: RWA market experiences structural growth, stablecoins bottom out at a $320 billion market, equities and commodities will become new growth drivers; Stablecoins enter the 2.0 era, from crypto payments to competing for global payment infrastructure; Stock tokenization liquidity may grow rapidly, DeFi integration will be key; Private lending RWA shifts to "asset-driven," and may accelerate differentiation under the pressure of "default" risk; Gold and commodities RWA Ushering in a new era of "full asset collateralization"; RWA liquidity will become more concentrated, and three types of RWA assets are gaining favor with exchanges; Crypto concept stocks are "rising," and DAT is "differentiating" and "concentrating." Figure 1. 7 Major Trends in Traditional Finance x Cryptocurrency Outlook for 2026 Figure 2. 8 Mega Forces Impacting Traditional Finance x Crypto in 2026 Part 1: Overview of TradeFi x Crypto in 2025 1.1 2025 Overview: The Tipping Point of Integration 2025 will be the year when traditional finance and crypto move from "parallel experiments" to "deep entanglement." If 2024 was the year of ETFs, then 2025 will be the year of "TradFi x Crypto backend reshaping." While the prices of native crypto assets remain volatile, blockchain-based clearing, settlement, and asset issuance (RWA) has quietly become a standard business unit for giants like BlackRock and JPMorgan Chase. Capital is no longer solely pursuing Beta returns, but rather the increased capital turnover brought about by tokenization. Key data achieved in 2025 are as follows: • Stablecoin Market Size Growth – Data: As of the end of December 2025, the total market size of stablecoins reached approximately $30 billion, representing a year-on-year growth of over 100% compared to the end of 2024 (approximately $13 billion). Stablecoin Annual Transaction Volume: In 2025, the total transaction amount of all stablecoins on the blockchain (USDC, USDT, PYUSD, EURC) reached $15.8 trillion, surpassing the annual processing volume of traditional retail payment giant Visa for the first time. Settlement penetration rate: In global cross-border foreign exchange (FX) clearing and settlement, "atomic settlement" based on regulated stablecoins accounts for 2.8%, and the penetration rate in the T+0 clearing field exceeds 12%. (Data Source: CoinFound/ Visa Crypto Analytics/ BIS (Project Guardian Quarterly Review)) • Total RWA Size (excluding stablecoins) and Growth – Data: As of the end of December 2025, the total RWA size (excluding stablecoins) exceeded US$2.5 billion, representing a year-on-year growth of over 100% compared to the end of 2024 (approximately US$1 billion). • Rise of Digital Asset Treasury (DAT) and Crypto Stocks – Data: In 2025, Digital Asset Treasury and crypto stocks emerged. As of the end of December 2025, the overall market capitalization of DAT alone was approximately US$224.1 billion. • Crypto ETF Size and Growth – Data: As of the end of December 2025, the total assets under management of global spot crypto ETFs (BTC, ETH, SOL, and mixed RWA ETFs) were approximately $140 billion, an increase of approximately 20% compared to the end of 2024 ($120 billion). 1.2 2025 TradFi x Crypto Key Events Timeline • January: BlackRock's BUIDL Fund and Circle's smart contract achieved interoperability upgrades, supporting 24/7 USDC subscriptions and redemptions, with T+0 settlement becoming a new industry benchmark. • February: The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) Project Guardian Expanding to Phase Four, Standard Chartered Bank and HSBC completed their first cross-currency repurchase transaction based on a public blockchain. • March: Stock tokenization received regulatory no-objection letters from relevant jurisdictions regarding the "non-trading-hour circulation of tokenized securities." • April: The first quarterly report after the full implementation of the EU's MiCA Act was released, showing a surge in the issuance of Euro stablecoins, surpassing the British pound to become the second largest on-chain lending currency. • May: The Swift Network announced the completion of its production environment integration with Chainlink CCIP, allowing member banks to transfer on-chain assets via existing Swift messaging instructions. • June: The US SEC approved the first ETF backed by a basket of RWA (US Treasury bonds + corporate bonds), marking the official entry of RWA assets into retail US stock accounts. • 7 • August: A mid-sized private lending RWA protocol faced a credit crisis due to opaque off-chain asset audits, prompting the industry to shift towards "real-time on-chain audit" standards. • August: The total market capitalization of gold tokenization surpassed $5 billion. Amid geopolitical tensions, PAXG and XAUt became the preferred safe-haven assets. • September: Payment giant Visa launched a "tokenized deposit adapter," directly connecting corporate bank accounts with public blockchain DeFi protocols. • October: The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission issued guidance on the integration of Security Token Offerings (STOs) and RWAs, allowing retail investors to participate within limits. • November: The total assets under management (AUM) of on-chain government bonds RWAs surpassed the $50 billion mark. • December: OpenAI... An invested energy company announced the issuance of a "Compute-backed Bond," ushering in a new wave of financialization of physical infrastructure (DePIN).

    Figure 3. A Review of Key Events in TradeFi x Crypto in 2025

    Part Two: The Mega Forces Influencing TradeFi x Crypto

    2.1 Macroeconomic uncertainty, inflation, and debt pressures persist, making safe-haven assets outside the fiat currency system more attractive. • The global fiat currency system has entered a phase of "slow credit erosion." – The "fiscal-driven" risk of US debt is intensifying: As of now, US federal debt has surpassed the $38 trillion mark (data source: U.S. Treasury Fiscal Data), and correspondingly, bond interest expenses have exceeded the defense budget, reaching over $1 trillion annually. Some argue that this debt spiral means the Federal Reserve cannot maintain high interest rates indefinitely (otherwise, interest rates would overwhelm the treasury), but lowering interest rates could trigger inflation. To hedge against this "fiat currency credit risk," institutions must allocate to hard assets with lower counterparty risk, such as gold or Bitcoin. Gold is becoming a consensus among central banks for "de-dollarization": gold is no longer merely a traditional anti-inflation tool, but is regarded by central banks as a strategic reserve for "de-dollarization" and "sanction resistance." According to data from the World Gold Council (WGC), global central banks have made net purchases of over 1,000 tons of gold for three consecutive years (2022-2024/25). This purchasing power mainly comes from emerging markets (China, Poland, Turkey, India, etc.). In particular, since Russia's foreign exchange reserves were frozen, global institutions have realized that "if your reserve assets are someone else's liabilities, then they don't truly belong to you." Gold is the only global reserve asset that does not rely on any government credit, thus becoming the preferred safe haven for sovereign funds. Real assets are beginning to become tools to combat "financial repression": In an environment of "financial repression" where inflation is consistently higher than interest rates, holding cash and bonds represents a loss in real purchasing power. In fact, the long-term inflation center is shifting upwards. Structural factors such as deglobalization (supply chain restructuring), energy transition (green inflation), and labor shortages make it difficult for inflation to return to 2%. The ideal target. Therefore, institutional portfolios (such as the 60/40 strategy) are losing appeal, and instead increasing allocations to commodities and scarce resources. These assets are outside the fiat currency system, and their value is anchored to physical supply and demand rather than government credit. • As long as the trend of global debt expansion does not reverse, institutions will continue to shift funds from "paper assets" (bonds, fiat currency) to "hard assets" (gold, Bitcoin, commodities). This is not just about hedging, but also a vote on trust in currency. Under this trend, – Bitcoin continues to undergo a narrative shift from "retail speculative product" to "institutional asset allocation." Traditional financial institutions no longer discuss whether to allocate, but how to achieve large-scale holdings through regulated channels (such as spot ETFs and their options products). – Gold, commodities, and other RWAs will see more innovative possibilities. 2.2 Fragmentation and Parallel Systems: The Implementation of Geopolitically Driven Blockchain Settlement • Geopolitical frictions have led to the fragmentation of the global payment market, making blockchain-based parallel settlement systems a substantial alternative for cross-border trade. • The need for "deweaponization" of financial infrastructure—moving away from SWIFT's single-point dependence: Geopolitical frictions (such as sanctions against Russia and restrictions on the use of the US dollar for settlement in some countries) have exposed the vulnerability of the highly centralized SWIFT network. Central banks around the world have realized the necessity of establishing a politically neutral settlement channel. Geopolitical tensions are forcing many countries to seek payment infrastructures independent of SWIFT, with BRICS countries and surrounding regions accelerating the development of digital currency payment agreements to mitigate the risks of sanctions (Source: BIS Review 2025/12). • mBridge and CBDC The Rise of Multilateral Bridges: Emerging economies are building multilateral settlement bridges through central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). This is the most typical manifestation of "fragmentation"—the world is no longer connected through a single global network, but has formed multiple regional settlement loops. – Atomic Settlement Solutions and Efficiency Improvements: In an environment of declining global trust, counterparty risk has risen sharply. Blockchain's smart contract capabilities solve the trust deadlock problem of "who transfers first." Furthermore, T+0 transaction and clearing solutions will unlock a large amount of funds in the clearing process, improving capital efficiency. Payment giants like Visa are beginning to utilize blockchain to achieve asset tokenization settlement, viewing it as key to increasing the speed of cross-border transactions and reducing the vulnerability of existing systems (Source: VisaEconomic Insights). • In the context of "geopolitical fragmentation of payments," it is foreseeable that: – Interoperability protocols will become the next generation of SWIFT: With The parallel existence of mBridge (central bank digital currency bridge), various bank private chains, and public chains will create countless "liquidity islands" in the financial market. Whoever can connect these islands will become the new SWIFT. The competition and integration between commercial bank tokenized deposits and crypto-native stablecoins may be ignited. Permissioned DeFi will rise. TradFi needs DeFi's Automated Market Makers (AMMs) and lending protocols to improve efficiency, but it cannot violate anti-money laundering (AML) regulations. Crypto is no longer just an alternative asset, but is seen by TradFi as the technological foundation for building parallel settlement systems. The following are the specific impacts of this trend on the integration of Crypto x TradFi. 2.3 AI Investment: From a Simple "Computing Power Race" to "Economic Productivity Generation" • Capital expenditure on AI is projected to surpass $500 billion in 2026, with AI and blockchain deeply converging at the levels of "value confirmation" and "automated execution." • Annual capital expenditure by major US tech companies has grown from $150 billion in 2023 to over $500 billion in 2026, and AI investment is contributing three times the historical average to US GDP (Source: BlackRock 2026 Outlook). These giants' hundreds of billions of dollars in capital expenditure (CapEx) must be converted into revenue. The market is no longer simply paying for "how many H100 graphics cards" but focusing on who can convert computing power into recurring revenue (ARR). This means that investment will shift massively from the hardware layer (Chips) to the application layer (Applications) and vertical models, seeking "killer applications" that can truly replace manual labor and reduce costs. Agentic AI may become central to a productivity explosion. The "Agentic Economy" may once again become a hot topic. AI can autonomously call APIs, book flights, write and deploy code, and generate income and expenditure. This shift transforms the value of AI from "improving efficiency" to "directly undertaking workflows," thereby generating substantial revenue contributions. It's worth noting that the scale of AI development is so large that even changes in capital expenditure at the micro level can trigger a chain reaction in the macro market (Source: J.P. Morgan). Similarly, the AI ​​trend will impact cryptocurrencies: – Native compliant bank accounts and KYC for AI agents will become key areas of discussion. Technically, AI agents can easily generate crypto wallets and use stablecoins for 24/7 resource procurement and economic activities; however, this depends on solving KYC issues. In the future, "compliant stablecoins" that meet the corresponding KYC and AML regulatory requirements may provide "AI..." The "custodial wallet" service will enter this emerging machine-to-machine (M2M) payment market, using agent-to-agent automated on-chain settlement to seize the B2B payment and DeFi landscape. 2.4 Energy is becoming key to AI and Mining • With computing power demand growing exponentially, "electricity" is replacing "computing power" as the new bottleneck in terms of shortage. Whether training AI models with trillions of parameters or maintaining the hash rate of the Bitcoin network, the ability to obtain stable, cheap, and large-scale energy access (Power Availability) is becoming an increasingly important topic. – By 2026, five data centers in the United States alone will each have electricity demands exceeding 1GW (equivalent to a nuclear reactor). It is projected that by 2028, the data center power shortage will reach 19GW, accounting for approximately 40% of total demand (Source: FT/S&P Global). Energy). Mining farms are transforming into "hybrid computing centers," flexibly switching between BTC mining and AI inference using surplus electricity to maximize revenue per unit of electricity. Some mining companies are choosing to tie themselves to AI giants through "long-term lease agreements," forming de facto joint ventures. On the other hand, leading mining companies (such as Marathon Digital and Riot Platforms) are using their abundant cash flow (from the Bitcoin bull market) for large-scale mergers and acquisitions, aiming not only to increase hash rate but also to control underlying energy assets. Energy bottlenecks are forcing Crypto and TradFi to revalue assets and structurally merge: – The valuation logic for mining companies is shifting from Crypto Proxy to Infra Asset: In the past, investors bought mining stocks (such as MARA and RIOT) as an indirect proxy for investing in cryptocurrencies, such as investing in Bitcoin and investing in Bitcoin-related mining stocks. Now, some mining companies are gradually gaining control of core power resources and transforming into… "Hybrid computing centers" imply a more stable discounted cash flow (DCF) model and a higher price-to-earnings ratio (P/E), reducing the impact of Bitcoin price volatility on stock prices. Therefore, they can be revalued using the logic of data center infrastructure or utility stocks. Meanwhile, mining companies may become a stable part of the "energy + computing power" allocation in a portfolio. TradFi and Big Tech companies may further directly acquire listed mining companies. The reason behind this is that grid expansion and the construction of new substations typically require a 3-5 year approval cycle, which tech giants cannot afford to wait for. The approved gigawatt-scale (GW) grid interconnection capacity (time-to-power) held by Bitcoin mining companies has become the scarcest spot asset in the market. Building gigawatt-scale energy facilities requires huge amounts of capital (CapEx), which crypto-native capital cannot afford to bear alone. We may see more RWA based on "computing power energy revenue". The discussion focused on the on-chain trading of future Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) or Carbon Credits. 2.5 Rapid Evolution of On-Chain Assets and the Future Financial System • 2026 is the year of "Programmable Finance," with traditional financial institutions redefining their middle and back-office processes through their own issued on-chain assets (RWAs). – The US GENIUS Act and the European DLT pilot program will enter full implementation in 2026, allowing sovereign debt, money market funds, etc., to be natively issued and cleared on public blockchains (Data source: Crypto Asset Lab 2026). – Asset tokenization shifts from "issuance" to "utility": T+0 The Liquidity and Collateral Revolution: Over the past two years, institutions have primarily focused on "how to put funds on-chain." The focus has now shifted to "what can be done after going on-chain." BlackRock's BUIDL and Franklin Templeton's funds are not merely digital certificates; they are being integrated into the broader financial ecosystem as 24/7 collateral. More similar attempts are emerging in areas such as stock tokenization (DTCC) and gold tokenization. For institutions, the intraday liquidity release in the repo market will be a highlight. The repo market is the "blood vessels" of the global financial system, but traditional processes are inefficient and costly due to overnight risk. JPMorgan's Onyx and Broadridge's DLR platforms demonstrate the significant advantages of blockchain in handling complex intraday repos. • Under this trend: The large-scale onboarding of existing assets will drive significant growth in sectors like RWA; Ethereum and Solana, among other high-performance public chains, are gradually evolving into universal settlement layers for global finance. In particular, privacy transactions will become a focus, with TradFi institutions processing them through Layer 2 or dedicated subnets. Composability will continue to face both challenges and opportunities from compliance: once TradFi assets are on-chain, the biggest attraction is leveraging the "Lego-like" nature of DeFi (e.g., trading tokenized stocks in Automated Market Makers (AMMs)). Compliance requirements will become the most important entry requirement. This could also spur an explosion of "permissioned DeFi." 2.6 Private Credit Enters a New Phase • In the past few years, private credit has experienced smooth sailing; however, in 2026... 2025-2026 may become the first year of stress testing for private lending. A large amount of private lending issued during the low-interest-rate period of 2020-2022 will mature in concentrated phases in 2025-2026. With the base rate surging from around 0% in 2020 to 4% currently, this could lead to increased interest payment pressure on companies. Many zombie companies held by private equity (PE) may be unable to repay their debts, causing a structural jump in default rates from their current "historically low" levels. On the other hand, a major selling point of private lending is its "low volatility," but this is often due to opaque and outdated valuations. As private lending products are increasingly sold to retail investors (through ETFs or BDCs), fund managers will face significant liquidity runs should the macroeconomy slow down in 2026 and retail investors attempt to redeem their investments. If the past boom in private lending provided assets for RWA, then 2026... The potential crisis of 2026 will be a test for the RWA model: – On-chain transparency becomes the "cure": The crisis will force funds to flow to more transparent RWA protocols. Protocols capable of "real-time monitoring of underlying assets" (e.g., uploading cash flow and inventory data of borrowing companies in real time via oracles) will prevail. The market will no longer trust simple "PDF reports" but will demand proof of solvency verified through zero-knowledge proofs (ZK). – Beware of the risk of "chain reaction liquidations" in DeFi lending protocols: If a wave of defaults erupts in the offline private lending market in 2026, the value of these RWA tokens will plummet. This will test the liquidation mechanisms and risk isolation capabilities of DeFi protocols. If not handled properly, offline defaults will spread to the on-chain via RWA, causing stablecoins to de-peg or lending protocols to default. This could lead to regulators imposing extremely strict "capital controls" on RWA. 2.7 Infrastructure Merger Moment: From "Fragmented Competition" to "Giant Consolidation" • In the 2025-2026 cycle, the crypto infrastructure is undergoing a consolidation period similar to the internet and telecommunications industry in the 2000s. Projects are being swallowed up, and giants are securing their positions through mergers and acquisitions. This trend will continue. The market is no longer paying for the "100th L2" or "new cross-chain bridges," and capital efficiency demands the elimination of redundancy. In particular, some trends are evident: – Payment giants will continue to devour "stablecoin middleware": The aftermath of Stripe's acquisition of Bridge. With Stripe's acquisition of Bridge ($1.1 billion) as the starting gun, traditional fintech giants realized that instead of building complex blockchain node networks from scratch, it was better to directly acquire mature "stablecoin API" infrastructure. Vertical Integration of Exchanges and Custodians: As fee wars intensify, pure transaction fee revenue is declining. Coinbase, Binance, and traditional asset management companies (such as Fidelity and BlackRock) are extending into upstream and downstream of the industry chain through mergers and acquisitions. 2.8 Room to Run for Emerging Markets: From Speculative Participation to Structural Dependence • In emerging markets (EM), cryptocurrencies such as Stablecoin and DeFi, along with their related applications, have enabled substantial financial services and are experiencing rapid growth. Some examples include: – Deepening Penetration: In regions such as India and Southeast Asia, cryptocurrencies are not only investment products but also underlying tools for payments and cross-border remittances. 76% of global investors plan to increase their exposure to digital assets in 2026, with emerging markets being a major growth driver (Source: CoinbaseInstitutional/CoinDCX). – Regulatory Compliance: Clearer rules gradually emerging in Asia (such as Singapore's MAS stablecoin framework) and Latin America have cleared obstacles for local institutions to participate in Crypto (Data source: B2Broker). This "structural dependence" in emerging markets provides the broadest "real-world user scenarios" and "asset distribution channels" for the integration of Crypto and TradFi. Emerging markets have become "application laboratories" for global crypto assets. Mature on-chain payment and credit models have first closed loops in these regions, subsequently feeding back into developed markets. Part Three: 2026 Trend Outlook 3.1 RWA Market to Grow 50% Annually, Stablecoins to Bottom Out at 320 Billion, Equities and Commodities to Become New Growth Drivers • Overall, the RWA industry is growing rapidly, with stablecoins still dominating. Non-Stablecoin Categories Within RWA, equity assets saw significant growth, with institutional alternative funds, stocks, and private equity (PE) experiencing explosive growth. Commodity RWA continued its accelerating trend. • RWA market capitalization grew by nearly 50% in 2025. At the beginning of 2025, RWA's market capitalization was close to $220 billion, and by the end of 2025, the RWA market size reached $320 billion. While stablecoins still accounted for the vast majority ($280 billion+), the growth rates of other asset classes are also noteworthy. Figure 4. Market Capitalization Comparison of Various RWA Sectors at the Beginning and End of 2025 (Source: CoinFound) • Among all RWA asset classes, Private Credit and US Treasury Debt ranked second (approximately $18 billion) and third (approximately $85 billion) in market capitalization, respectively. Private Credit accounted for more than half of the market capitalization of non-stablecoin RWAs starting in 2022. Despite the significant rise of US Treasury Debts in 2023-2024, data proves that Private Credit is the dominant non-stablecoin RWA. It has been a long-term cornerstone. Since 2022, it has consistently held more than half of the market share, reaching a market capitalization of $18 billion by the end of 2025. This demonstrates that on-chain lending has been deeply embedded in real-world financing, forming a stable lending ecosystem. Bonds, on the other hand, experienced rapid growth in 2023-2024, thanks to market education through the high-interest environment. • In the non-stablecoin RWA sector, the growth drivers began to shift in 2025: – In 2025, institutional funds and institutional alternative funds, stocks, and private equity saw significant growth in market capitalization share. Furthermore, these categories became new growth drivers in 2025; in contrast, in 2024, the growth of non-stablecoin RWA was primarily driven by private lending RWA and US Treasury RWA. – Furthermore, RWA in the commodities category continued to achieve rapid growth in every quarter of 2025, achieving a 300% increase throughout the year (from a market capitalization of $1 billion to $3 billion), with the growth rate showing a trend of accelerating quarter by quarter. Figure 5. Market capitalization share of various RWA sub-sectors (excluding stablecoins) from 2022 to 2025 (Data source: CoinFound) Figure 6. Market capitalization growth of various RWA sectors (excluding stablecoins) from 2022 to 2025 (Source: CoinFound) Figure 7. Market capitalization growth of various RWA sectors (excluding stablecoins) in 2025 (Source: CoinFound) Market capitalization growth of the sector (excluding stablecoins) (Data source: CoinFound)

    3.2 From Stablecoin 2.0, from Crypto Payments to Global Payment Infrastructure and Interest Generation

    • In 2025, stablecoins officially shed their single label as "crypto settlement tools" and evolved into the digital capillaries of global financial circulation. As of the end of December, the total market capitalization of stablecoins exceeded $250 billion, and their annual settlement volume historically surpassed the Visa system, marking that the settlement layer based on public chains has the strength to compete with traditional financial giants.

    Figure 11. Market capitalization of various private lending RWA issuance platforms from 2022 to 2025 (Data source: CoinFound)

    85%). (Weight), as the "traffic engine" of the entire network settlement, has an extremely high turnover rate and is an extension of the on-chain dollar. – Stock RWA (turnover rate 15%-25%): As a "yield gamer," driven by the volatility of US stocks, it has performed exceptionally well on Bybit and Kraken, and is key to attracting retail arbitrage traffic. – US Treasury bonds and credit (turnover rate <5%): As a "credit ballast," although the trading frequency is low, it can be used as an underlying asset in lending protocols. These assets are gradually evolving into "core on-chain collateral," undergoing a profound evolution from "earning yield" to "collateralization." • Correspondingly, exchange strategies are beginning to diverge: – Leveraging compliance advantages to monopolize the institutional US Treasury and USDC markets: Although Coinbase ranks 5th in trading volume, its weighting is highly concentrated in USDC (84%) and institutional US Treasury (12%), demonstrating its deep cultivation of the compliance and institutional markets. – Shifting towards panoramic exchanges through aggressive RWA deployments to compete for global users: Bybit, Kraken, and Gate.io are very aggressive in their RWA deployments. Bybit, in particular, has RWA as its primary weighting (46%), indicating that these exchanges are attracting retail traffic seeking on-chain trading of US/global stocks. – The power of DEX and DeFi: Uniswap and Curve entering the top 10 signifies that RWA is no longer limited to CEXs. In particular, Uniswap's trading volume ($2.6 billion+) in interest-bearing stablecoins and US Treasuries is comparable to top-tier centralized exchanges. High-turnover assets (stablecoins, stocks) support the exchange's market share and activity; while low-turnover assets (US Treasuries, private lending), although having low "turnover rates" in the secondary market, represent the most stable underlying capital inflow in the on-chain ecosystem. The trend in 2026 will be more low-turnover assets releasing secondary liquidity through "collateralization." Turnover rate (Velocity) is a key indicator for measuring the "health" of asset liquidity. In RWA's research report, turnover rate helps us identify which assets are "real trading hotspots." In this research report, we define asset turnover as: – Velocity = (Daily Volume) (Circulating Market Cash) – High turnover: indicates assets are frequently traded, with excellent liquidity, suitable for speculation or as a short-term payment medium. – Low turnover: indicates assets are mostly held long-term (HODL) or used as collateral, with low liquidity.
